stories.title: The European Wanderer
stories.intended_reader: Those who sailed Splendour during her European and transatlantic years
stories.core_insight: Splendour specialized in European cruising and became beloved for Mediterranean and transatlantic itineraries
stories.markdown:
**Splendour of the Seas launched in April 1996 as the sixth and final Vision-class ship, and I remember the exact moment she became my ship—not from a brochure or a booking confirmation, but from a photograph my mother showed me of Splendour sailing out of Barcelona at sunset, her glass walls catching Mediterranean gold in a way that made the ship look like she belonged to that coastline rather than visiting it.**

Where her Vision-class sisters—[Grandeur](/ships/rcl/grandeur-of-the-seas.html), [Rhapsody](/ships/rcl/rhapsody-of-the-seas.html), [Vision](/ships/rcl/vision-of-the-seas.html), [Enchantment](/ships/rcl/enchantment-of-the-seas.html)—carved their reputations in Caribbean and Alaskan waters, Splendour chose a different identity from her earliest deployments: European specialist, Mediterranean wanderer, transatlantic crosser. She spent her Royal Caribbean career sailing waters that older civilizations had navigated for millennia, and her glass-wrapped design made those ancient coastlines feel like they were aboard the ship rather than passing outside it.

The specifications told one story: 69,130 gross tons, 1,804 passengers at double occupancy, 867 feet from bow to stern, built by Chantiers de l'Atlantique in France. But specifications never capture personality. Splendour's personality was European to her core—elegant without pretension, cultured without snobbery, sized perfectly for ports that mega-ships would later overwhelm.

Her primary life was Mediterranean summers. Barcelona to Rome. Greek islands to Adriatic ports. Western Mediterranean loops that visited Mallorca, Corsica, and the Italian Riviera in single voyages. She'd reposition across the Atlantic in spring and fall, giving transatlantic enthusiasts seven consecutive sea days of uninterrupted shipboard life—a crossing that felt like meditation disguised as transportation.

I first boarded Splendour in the summer of 2004 for a Mediterranean sailing that changed my understanding of what cruise ships could be. I'd cruised Caribbean routes on larger ships and found the experience pleasant but disconnected—the ship felt like a resort that happened to float, and the destinations felt like interruptions to the resort experience. Splendour reversed that equation entirely. The ship existed to serve the destinations, and the destinations were magnificent.

The seven-story Centrum atrium with its glass elevators created a sense of vertical space that made the ship feel larger than her tonnage suggested. Viking Crown Lounge, perched at the top of the ship and wrapped entirely in glass, became my evening ritual—sunset over whatever Mediterranean coastline we were sailing past, viewed from 360 degrees with a drink in hand and conversation flowing with fellow passengers who'd chosen Splendour for the same reason I had: she fit Europe the way European ships should.

Splendour could dock in Santorini's harbor when mega-ships anchored offshore and tendered. She could navigate Dubrovnik's old port when larger vessels diverted to industrial terminals miles from the Old City. She could sail directly into Venice through the lagoon—St. Mark's Square growing from postcard miniature to living reality as the ship approached—a privilege that Venice's later cruise restrictions would eventually curtail but that Splendour's passengers experienced as routine magic.

The ship served Europe from 1996 until Royal Caribbean sold her in 2016—twenty years of Mediterranean summers and transatlantic crossings that built a loyal following of passengers who understood that mid-sized ships offered advantages in European waters that no amount of mega-ship spectacle could replicate. She became Marella Discovery under TUI Cruises, still sailing European waters under a different name, her glass walls and Viking Crown Lounge preserved for a new generation of passengers who may never know her original name but still benefit from her original design philosophy.

**The lesson:** Some ships find their purpose not in being the biggest or newest but in being perfectly matched to the waters they sail—and Splendour's European career proved that right-sized means right for everything that matters.**

stories.title: What Splendour Passengers Valued
stories.intended_reader: Splendour loyalists who preferred her European itineraries
stories.core_insight: Splendour proved that mid-sized ships offered advantages mega-ships couldn't match for European cruising
stories.markdown:
**At seventy-two, with nineteen Splendour sailings behind me, I've earned the authority to explain what made this ship irreplaceable—not through statistics or professional review, but through the accumulated evidence of two decades spent watching her do things no larger ship could accomplish in the waters she served best.**

My wife and I discovered Splendour in 1998, two years after her launch, on a Mediterranean sailing that our travel agent had recommended as "a nice European option." Nice. That word became our family joke for years afterward, applied to anything so spectacularly understated that its true quality required experience rather than description. Splendour wasn't nice. She was perfect for what she did, and what she did was European cruising at a scale that honored the destinations rather than overwhelming them.

The mid-sized advantage wasn't theoretical—it was practical in ways we experienced at every port. Splendour could dock where [Oasis class](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html) ships couldn't reach. She'd tie up directly in Mediterranean harbors that mega-ships viewed from industrial ports miles away, and the difference between walking off a ship into a medieval city and riding a shuttle bus to its outskirts is the difference between visiting a place and merely being near it.

Dubrovnik was our annual proof. Splendour docked close enough to the Old City that we could walk to the Pile Gate in fifteen minutes, arriving before the mega-ship tender crowds materialized. We'd have two hours of nearly empty streets before the first wave of thousands arrived from larger ships anchored offshore. Two hours of Dubrovnik to ourselves—not because we'd paid for exclusive access but because our ship could reach the destination directly.

The right-sized principle extended beyond ports to the onboard experience. At 1,804 passengers, Splendour carried enough people for genuine social variety—we met fascinating travelers from every continent across our nineteen sailings—but few enough that the crew learned our names, the dining staff remembered our preferences, and the ship felt like a community rather than a population.

Transatlantic crossings aboard Splendour were bucket-list achievements we repeated six times. Seven days at sea, formal nights that felt earned rather than obligatory, enrichment lectures that respected our intelligence, and the particular peace of knowing that no port was approaching, no excursion demanded booking, no alarm would wake us—just the ship, the ocean, and the specific luxury of days without destination.

The glass walls that defined Vision Class design served European cruising magnificently. Norwegian fjord sailings put waterfalls close enough to mist the outer decks while we watched through windows warm enough for shirtsleeves. Greek coastlines slid past the Windjammer during breakfast. The Viking Crown Lounge at sunset, Mediterranean gold flooding every surface through 360 degrees of glass—that was our evening ritual across nineteen sailings, and it never diminished through repetition.

When Royal Caribbean sold Splendour in 2016, European cruisers lost something that fleet modernization couldn't replace. Newer ships are technically superior in nearly every measurable category. They're also too large for half the ports Splendour visited, too crowded for the atmosphere she cultivated, and too focused on onboard entertainment to prioritize the destination-first philosophy she embodied.

The woman who'd sold us our first Splendour sailing—our travel agent, now retired—sent us a note when the sale was announced. "Some ships are irreplaceable," she wrote. "The rest are just ships." After nineteen sailings, we understood exactly what she meant.

**The lesson:** The ships that earn loyalty don't compete on size or novelty—they prove, over decades and countless port calls, that being right-sized for the places that matter is worth more than any amount of onboard spectacle.**

stories.title: My First Transatlantic: Seven Days at Sea
stories.intended_reader: Travelers considering repositioning cruises or who enjoy sea days
stories.core_insight: Splendour's transatlantic crossings offered a unique cruise experience focused on shipboard life rather than ports
stories.markdown:
**I booked Splendour's spring repositioning cruise from Barcelona to Fort Lauderdale for the price—transatlantic crossings are cruising's great bargain—and discovered something I hadn't anticipated: seven consecutive sea days that became the most meditative, restorative, and unexpectedly social experience of my traveling life.**

Most cruisers want ports. They want beach days and shore excursions and photographs proving they've stood in famous places. They treat the ship as transportation between destinations—a necessary conveyance that earns its keep by delivering them to interesting geography. I'd been that cruiser for six previous sailings. The ship was the means; the ports were the end.

Transatlantic crossings reverse that equation so completely that the first two days feel disorienting. The ship IS the destination. No ports. No excursions. No alarm clocks set for early tender departures. Just you, the Atlantic Ocean stretching to every horizon, and seven days of uninterrupted shipboard life that gradually reveal how much of your daily existence is structured by obligation rather than desire.

Splendour was ideally sized for the crossing. Small enough to feel like a community—by day three, I recognized faces in the corridors and had developed nodding relationships with a dozen fellow passengers. Large enough to never feel trapped—multiple bars, lounges, deck spaces, the library, the pool deck, the Solarium, the Viking Crown Lounge provided sufficient variety that routine emerged without monotony.

My daily rhythm established itself by day two and deepened through the remaining five days. Wake naturally—no alarm, no port schedule dictating consciousness. Coffee on the balcony, watching the Atlantic perform its daily mood: glassy calm one morning, whitecapped and dramatic the next, always enormous, always reminding me that the ocean was conducting our crossing on its own terms regardless of what the ship's schedule suggested.

Breakfast at whatever pace morning dictated—sometimes the Windjammer's buffet for efficiency, sometimes the main dining room for lingering service and better coffee. Morning enrichment lectures occupied the mid-morning hours: navigation officers explaining how modern ships cross the Atlantic versus historical sailing routes, maritime historians describing the ocean's role in connecting civilizations, destination briefers preparing us for Fort Lauderdale arrival as if we needed preparation for Florida's most familiar cruise port.

Afternoons belonged to the pool deck or the library, depending on weather and inclination. The library became my unexpected sanctuary—small, quiet, stocked with actual books rather than the digital screens that had replaced reading in my daily life. I read four novels across seven days, a quantity I hadn't achieved in a single month for years. The Atlantic's rhythm—the gentle ship motion, the play of light through windows, the absence of digital interruption—restored a reading attention span I'd assumed was permanently degraded.

The people made the crossing transformative rather than merely pleasant. Transatlantic cruisers are different from port-to-port passengers—self-selected for the specific experience of sustained time at sea rather than rapid destination sampling. They tend toward retirement, experience, and the particular intelligence that comes from decades of travel and reflection. Conversations at the bar, at dinner, on deck watching dolphins arc through the ship's bow wave—these conversations went deeper and lasted longer than any I'd experienced on port-intensive cruises.

I met a couple from London on day two over morning coffee. By day five, we were sharing life stories, professional disappointments, personal victories, and the kind of intimate observations that typically require years of friendship to unlock. The crossing's isolation created intimacy the way snowstorms create community—by removing alternatives and forcing genuine engagement with the people around you.

Viking Crown Lounge at sunset became the social fulcrum—passengers gathering as the Atlantic turned gold and then dark, drinks in hand, conversation flowing with the particular ease of people who've spent enough days together at sea to move past small talk into substance. I stayed until the last person left on three of seven evenings, reluctant to end conversations that felt like they mattered.

When we docked in Fort Lauderdale, I didn't want to leave. The ocean had become familiar in a way that land hadn't felt in years. The ship had become home in a way that my apartment rarely achieved. And the people I'd met—temporary community bound by shared time at sea—had become friends I still email, still visit, still miss.

**The lesson:** Seven days at sea isn't emptiness—it's the specific abundance that appears when you stop filling time with destinations and start experiencing the journey itself as the destination worth savoring.**

stories.title: Venice to Barcelona: Mediterranean Magic
stories.core_insight: Splendour's size allowed access to intimate Mediterranean ports that larger ships couldn't reach
stories.markdown:
**Our honeymoon cruise on Splendour started in Venice, and I need you to understand what that means—not departing from a port near Venice, not shuttling to a terminal in Venice's industrial outskirts, but sailing directly through the Venetian lagoon while St. Mark's Square shrank behind us, the basilica's golden domes catching evening light, gondolas bobbing in our gentle wake. My new husband held my hand at the rail, and neither of us spoke for twenty minutes because there was nothing language could add to the moment.**

We chose Splendour specifically because she could dock in Venice proper. The mega-ships—[Oasis class](/ships/rcl/oasis-of-the-seas.html) and their descendants—anchored at industrial ports and required water taxis or shuttle buses into the city. Splendour sailed into the Venice cruise terminal itself. Walk off the ship, walk into history. That directness—the absence of logistics between vessel and destination—defined our entire honeymoon experience.

The pattern repeated at every port on our Mediterranean routing, and each time, Splendour's size proved its value.

Dubrovnik: we docked at the cruise port within walking distance of the Old City walls. No tender boats required, no shuttles, no wasted time sitting in queues while our limited port hours ticked away. We were walking the ancient walls above the Adriatic within thirty minutes of the gangway opening, morning light making the red rooftops glow while the city still belonged to its residents rather than the cruise tourist crowds that would arrive later from larger ships anchored offshore.

Santorini: yes, this port required a tender, but Splendour's smaller passenger count meant shorter queues and faster turnaround. We had morning coffee watching the caldera's impossible blue from the tender dock, then spent the entire day exploring Oia's white-washed paths without fighting through crowds. By the time the mega-ship tender waves arrived in late morning, we'd already found the quiet cafe with the perfect caldera view that every tourist photograph promises but crowded conditions rarely deliver.

Barcelona: docked at the main terminal, walked to Las Ramblas within minutes. The city felt accessible, not distant. We wandered Gaudí's architectural fantasies, ate tapas at a counter where the chef spoke no English and we spoke no Spanish but the food translated everything we needed to communicate, and returned to the ship at sunset with tired feet and full hearts.

Why size mattered to our honeymoon wasn't just about port access—though that advantage alone justified our choice. Splendour at 69,130 gross tons carried 1,804 passengers. The mega-ships carried five or six thousand. The difference in port experience was the difference between visiting a destination and invading it. We never felt like an occupying force. Ports felt welcoming rather than bracing for impact. Local restaurants could accommodate us without three-hour waits. Cobblestone streets retained their romance rather than becoming crowd-management corridors.

The romance aboard matched the romance ashore. Every evening, we'd watch sunset from Viking Crown Lounge—360-degree glass walls turning the Mediterranean into a light show that no theater could replicate. Gold, then rose, then purple, then the particular deep blue that Mediterranean twilight produces in the hour before stars appear. Other couples had discovered the same magic; we made friends with three pairs who became our dinner companions for the voyage, honeymoon solidarity creating instant friendship.

Formal nights in the main dining room felt elegant and earned—we'd dressed for each other, for the occasion, for the ship that was carrying us through waters that had inspired romance for millennia. Our server recognized our honeymoon status by night two and treated every dinner as celebration. Wine appeared unprompted. Desserts arrived with congratulatory notes. The maitre d' sent champagne to our table the final evening, and the sommelier personally selected a bottle from our wedding year with a note reading: "For the beginning of everything."

We keep that note in our wedding album beside the photograph of us at Viking Crown Lounge, Mediterranean sunset blazing behind us, grinning with the specific joy of two people who'd chosen exactly the right way to begin their marriage.

Splendour is gone from Royal Caribbean now, but [Brilliance](/ships/rcl/brilliance-of-the-seas.html) and [Serenade](/ships/rcl/serenade-of-the-seas.html) still offer right-sized Mediterranean experiences. The ports haven't changed. The sunsets haven't dimmed. And the principle Splendour taught us—that smaller ships create bigger moments in places where history matters more than horsepower—remains as true now as it was on our honeymoon.

**The lesson:** The best honeymoon doesn't require the biggest ship or the most exotic destination—it requires a vessel humble enough to serve the places it visits rather than competing with them for your attention.**

stories.title: The Glass Walls That Defined a Class
stories.intended_reader: Ship enthusiasts interested in Vision Class design history
stories.core_insight: Vision Class ships, including Splendour, pioneered glass-wall design that influenced all future Royal Caribbean ships
stories.markdown:
**Vision Class ships weren't simply vessels—they were architectural statements that changed how the entire cruise industry thought about the relationship between passengers and the sea. Splendour of the Seas exemplified everything the class represented, and I discovered this not as a design professional or industry analyst but as a first-time cruiser who boarded expecting a floating hotel and encountered a floating greenhouse instead.**

My previous conception of cruise ships came from movies and my parents' stories—interior-focused spaces with small portholes, dark wood paneling, and the general aesthetic of an upscale shopping mall that happened to rock gently. The ocean was something you went outside to see, competing with wind and weather for the privilege.

When Royal Caribbean launched Legend of the Seas in 1995 as the first Vision-class ship, the industry noticed something fundamentally different: glass everywhere. Not decorative accent windows or strategically placed portholes, but structural commitment to transparency that treated the ocean as the ship's primary design element. Floor-to-ceiling windows in the dining room. Glass elevators in the atrium. Viking Crown Lounge wrapped entirely in glass at the ship's highest public point. Even the fitness center had ocean views, as if the designers believed that no activity aboard should be disconnected from the water below.

Splendour, launched the following year in 1996, perfected the concept that Legend had introduced. By the time I boarded her in 2003 for a Mediterranean sailing, the glass wall philosophy had been refined through fleet experience into something approaching architectural doctrine.

The seven-story Centrum atrium was my first encounter with Vision Class thinking. Glass elevators ascending through the heart of the ship, natural light flooding every level from a skylight that turned the interior into a column of illumination. I rode those elevators three times before finding my cabin—not because I was lost but because the experience of rising through light while the ocean appeared and disappeared through shifting glass panels was too captivating to interrupt with practical concerns like locating my stateroom.

Viking Crown Lounge became my obsession. Perched at the ship's apex, wrapped entirely in glass, offering 360-degree views that transformed every sunset into a private symphony of light. The Mediterranean performed differently through those windows each evening—Adriatic blue shifting to gold, Greek island silhouettes darkening against orange skies, Corsican mountains catching final light while the sea below turned from sapphire to ink. I spent every evening of a twelve-night sailing in that lounge, and each sunset was entirely different from the last.

The Windjammer Café continued the philosophy into daily meals. Wall-to-wall windows turned breakfast into theater—Mediterranean coastlines or Atlantic horizons as the backdrop to scrambled eggs and coffee, the ordinary act of eating transformed by extraordinary views that most restaurants would charge premium prices to offer.

Even the corridors participated. Windows at each end of every hallway provided natural orientation—you could always find the ocean, always know which side of the ship you were on, always maintain the awareness that you were sailing through the world rather than sealed away from it. I never felt lost aboard Splendour. The ocean was my compass.

The design legacy extends far beyond Vision Class. Every Royal Caribbean ship since has incorporated glass design elements inspired by what Vision Class proved: that passengers want connection to the sea, not protection from it. The AquaTheater's open-air performance space on Oasis Class. The North Star observation pod on Quantum Class rising above the ship on a glass-enclosed mechanical arm. The Overlook Bar with its glass-bottom floor on Icon Class. Each innovation traces its philosophical ancestry to the moment Vision Class declared that cruise ships should be transparent rather than opaque.

Splendour and her sisters didn't merely carry passengers across oceans. They changed the architectural conversation about what a cruise ship should feel like from the inside—and the answer, proven across thirty years and billions of dollars of subsequent construction, was that it should feel like being inside the view.

**The lesson:** The most influential design innovations don't call attention to themselves—they change the relationship between people and their environment so fundamentally that the previous approach becomes unimaginable, and Vision Class glass walls accomplished exactly that for an entire industry.**

stories.title: Accessible Cruising on Splendour: What Worked
stories.intended_reader: Travelers with mobility needs researching historical accessibility standards
stories.core_insight: Splendour's compact layout made wheelchair navigation easier than on larger, more sprawling ships
stories.markdown:
**My father used a wheelchair for the last twelve years of his life, and we cruised Splendour of the Seas three times together before she was sold to TUI in 2016. Here's what worked, what didn't, and what I wish every cruise line understood about the difference between accessibility compliance and genuine welcome—a distinction Dad recognized instantly and that determined whether a vacation felt like living or merely being accommodated.**

Dad had been a carpenter before the accident—a man who built houses with his hands, who measured his worth in physical capability, who struggled for years with a wheelchair that represented everything he'd lost. Cruising was Mom's idea. She believed that travel might restore something the accident had taken beyond mobility—the joy of encountering new experiences, the confidence of navigating unfamiliar territory, the sense that the world still contained surprises worth the effort of reaching them.

Splendour proved Mom right, though the evidence accumulated gradually across three voyages rather than arriving in a single dramatic revelation.

**What worked brilliantly:**

The compact layout was Splendour's greatest accessibility advantage, and it's one that mega-ship advocates consistently underestimate. The ship was small enough that Dad could navigate independently. Cabin to dining room: five minutes, no wrong turns, no corridor mazes requiring map consultation. Cabin to pool deck: three minutes. Cabin to Viking Crown Lounge: one elevator ride. Everything was reachable without the energy expenditure that larger ships demanded, and for wheelchair users whose daily energy budget is finite and precious, that efficiency translated directly into more time experiencing the ship and less time traversing it.

Elevators were positioned centrally and, crucially, were rarely overcrowded. Dad's wait time averaged under two minutes during normal operating hours. On larger ships we'd tried previously, elevator waits during peak periods—disembarkation mornings, show times, dinner rushes—reached ten to fifteen minutes, with full elevators passing Dad's floor repeatedly because there wasn't room for a wheelchair among the standing passengers. Splendour's mid-sized passenger count meant elevator competition stayed manageable, and Dad never had to watch a full elevator pass him by more than once.

The accessible cabin was functional rather than luxurious, but functional mattered more. Wide doorways that his wheelchair cleared without scraping. Roll-in shower with a bench at the right height and grab bars positioned for lateral transfer rather than the vertical-only support that many "accessible" bathrooms provide. Lowered closet rods he could reach independently. A balcony large enough for the wheelchair plus a chair for Mom beside him. They'd sit together in the evenings watching Mediterranean light change, Dad's hand on the railing of a ship he couldn't walk but could sail.

The crew made the critical difference. Small ship meant personal recognition—they learned Dad's name, his routine, his preferences, and his pride. Our cabin steward knew when to have the room ready without being told. The dining staff had his preferred table prepared before arrival—positioned for wheelchair access without being conspicuously separated from other guests. The bartender at Viking Crown Lounge poured his scotch and had it waiting by the time Dad's elevator delivered him. These weren't accommodations; they were relationships, and relationships require the intimacy that only smaller ships consistently provide.

**What didn't work:**

Tender ports caused genuine difficulty. Santorini, Montenegro, and some Greek island stops required transferring from ship to tender boat—a process that involved lifting Dad's wheelchair across a gap between moving platforms. The crew managed it with skill and determination, but the experience was stressful, undignified, and occasionally frightening for a man who'd already lost enough control over his physical circumstances. We skipped tender ports on our second and third voyages, choosing to enjoy the ship while tendered ports operated—and discovering that Splendour at anchor, with reduced passenger counts aboard, offered its own quiet pleasures.

Pool access lacked a lift. The Solarium was somewhat more accessible but still required transfers that Dad found difficult. He mostly watched from poolside, which he claimed to prefer—though I suspect he said so to spare us the guilt of swimming while he sat. That's the invisible cost of incomplete accessibility: not just the activities you can't do, but the emotional labor of pretending you don't mind.

A few bars and lounges had steps without ramps. The casino spacing was tight. The library aisles were narrow. These limitations were products of their era rather than deliberate exclusion, but they constrained Dad's independent exploration in ways that the ship's overall accessibility otherwise minimized.

**The honest assessment:**

Splendour wasn't perfect for wheelchair users. No 1996-built ship meets current accessibility expectations. But her compact size made her more manageable than mega-ships we'd tried, and the crew's genuine attentiveness compensated for design limitations that no amount of post-construction renovation could fully resolve. Dad felt welcomed, cared for, and known—and for a man whose disability had made him feel invisible in too many spaces ashore, being known mattered more than being perfectly accommodated.

**The lesson:** Accessibility isn't a checklist of features—it's the sum of design, scale, and human attention that determines whether a traveler with disability feels like an honored guest or a problem to be managed, and Splendour's crew consistently chose honor.**

stories.title: Norwegian Fjords: Why Splendour Was Built for This
stories.core_insight: Splendour's glass-wall design and smaller size made her ideal for scenic cruising through narrow waterways
stories.markdown:
**Some ships are built for activities—waterslides, rock walls, go-karts, the escalating arms race of onboard entertainment designed to make you forget you're at sea. Splendour of the Seas was built for views, and the Norwegian fjords proved it so convincingly that we sailed the same itinerary three consecutive summers without any of us—including two children who typically demanded novelty like oxygen—ever suggesting we try something different.**

The first sailing happened by accident. We'd wanted a Mediterranean cruise, but scheduling conflicts pushed us toward Splendour's fjord itinerary as a fallback. "Norway will be fine," my wife said, with the particular enthusiasm of someone managing expectations. Neither of us anticipated that "fine" would become "transformative," or that our accidental destination would become the family's most fiercely protected annual tradition.

The fjords are narrow waterways where geography performs at close range. Waterfalls cascade from cliff faces so near the ship that mist reaches the outer decks. Rock walls rise thousands of feet on both sides, creating corridors of stone through which the ship navigates with a precision that makes you newly aware of the crew's skill. You don't want to be inside during fjord transits—you want to see, to hear, to feel the scale of what water has carved through mountain over millennia.

Splendour's glass walls meant we could do exactly that from almost any public space aboard. Breakfast in the Windjammer with Geirangerfjord sliding past the floor-to-ceiling windows—my daughter's cereal spoon frozen halfway to her mouth as a waterfall appeared so close she instinctively leaned back from the glass. Lunch on the pool deck as mountains rose on both sides and mist created rainbows in the ship's wake. Dinner in the main dining room while the sun set over peaks that wouldn't experience full darkness during Norway's summer months, golden light persisting past midnight in a display that made the concept of "evening" meaningless.

The navigation moments became our family's shared rituals. The captain would announce scenic highlights over the PA system—"Ladies and gentlemen, we are approaching the Seven Sisters waterfall on the port side"—and the ship would come alive with movement as passengers rushed to viewing positions. On Splendour, "viewing position" meant everywhere. The glass walls ensured that no matter where you stood—restaurant, lounge, corridor, elevator—Norway was visible, available, demanding your attention with the quiet authority of landscapes that have been demanding attention since the last ice age retreated.

Splendour's smaller size unlocked passages and destinations that mega-ships couldn't attempt. We visited villages accessible only by sea, communities of a few hundred people who received their supplies by boat and their visitors by ships small enough to navigate their harbors. We docked at piers that couldn't have supported a vessel twice Splendour's tonnage—wooden structures built for fishing boats and ferries, suddenly hosting a cruise ship that felt, at these scales, positively enormous despite being modest by industry standards.

The family experience evolved across our three summers in ways I hadn't predicted. My kids—ten and fourteen on our first fjord sailing—had grown up on Caribbean cruises where the ship's pool and the ship's activities defined their vacation experience. Norway changed their relationship with cruising fundamentally. The scenery entertained them more thoroughly than any waterslide could, because the scenery was real, was massive, and was changing constantly.

Watching a waterfall from close enough to feel its mist on your face. Seeing glaciers calve ice into fjord waters that had been gathering cold since before human civilization existed. Spotting seals lounging on rocks as the ship passed close enough for photography. The natural world, performing without script or schedule, provided entertainment that no cruise director could match—and my children recognized this without being told, which is the surest evidence that the experience was genuinely compelling rather than parentally imposed.

By the third summer, my daughter—now sixteen—requested Norway specifically over every other destination available. "It's not about the ship, Mom," she said with the matter-of-fact wisdom of a teenager stating what adults should have understood all along. "It's about what's outside the ship."

That's what Splendour understood. That's what her glass walls declared with every square foot of transparency. The ship wasn't the destination. She was the window to the destination. And through that window, Norway performed miracles that no onboard activity could approximate.

**The lesson:** The best family cruise ship isn't the one with the most activities—it's the one that makes the world outside so visible and immediate that children voluntarily look up from their screens and adults voluntarily forget about their schedules, and Splendour's glass walls accomplished both effortlessly.**

stories.title: A Veteran's Mediterranean Memories
stories.core_insight: Splendour's European itineraries allowed veterans to revisit significant historical sites in comfort
stories.markdown:
**I served in the United States Navy for thirty years. I saw the Mediterranean from the bridge of destroyers, the deck of aircraft carriers, and the cramped quarters of submarines. I saw it through binoculars, through targeting systems, through portholes designed for function rather than beauty. Sailing it on Splendour of the Seas was healing I didn't know I needed, in a way I couldn't have predicted and my wife had quietly planned.**

My wife convinced me to try cruising after retirement. I was skeptical—crowds, confined spaces, enforced leisure, the particular torture of being told to "have fun" by people in uniform. These concerns weren't theoretical; they were the accumulated anxiety of a career spent in tight quarters where confined spaces meant danger and crowds meant operational complexity. The civilian world's version of life at sea sounded like my professional life without the purpose that made it bearable.

She suggested Splendour's Mediterranean itinerary because she knew the ports from our shore-leave visits decades ago. She also knew—with the particular insight that thirty-two years of marriage develops—that I needed to see those places differently. Not as a sailor on mission. Not as an officer scanning for threats. As a retired man who'd earned the right to simply experience beauty without calculating its strategic significance.

Naples was the first port that broke through my defenses. I'd pulled into Naples harbor dozens of times on warships—always on duty, always focused on operational requirements, never actually seeing the city beyond its military utility. On Splendour, we toured Pompeii. My wife held my hand as I stood in ruins I'd sailed past for decades without visiting, history I'd been stationed adjacent to without ever touching. The emotional weight of finally being present—not deployed, not assigned, but present by choice—overwhelmed me in ways I hadn't anticipated and couldn't fully control.

Dubrovnik carried different weight. I'd tracked naval movements along the Adriatic during the Yugoslav conflicts—real operations with real consequences, the coastline of Croatia carrying associations that tourist brochures couldn't erase. Walking the Old City walls with my grandchildren, pointing out architectural details while deliberately not mentioning what I'd monitored from those same waters decades earlier, I felt the past and present coexist in my chest like competing heartbeats. My granddaughter asked why the city had been rebuilt after the war. I told her because beautiful things deserve restoration. I was talking about more than architecture.

Barcelona brought the closest thing to peace. I'd anchored offshore during NATO exercises—the city a backdrop to operational briefings rather than a destination. This time I tasted paella at a tiny restaurant where the chef spoke no English and I spoke no Spanish but the food communicated everything words couldn't. I wandered Gaudí's architecture with the specific amazement of someone seeing impossible beauty after years of functional design. I sat in quiet plazas where the loudest sound was fountain water, and I listened to silence the way I'd once listened for threats—attentively, gratefully, with full awareness that silence is never guaranteed.

Splendour worked for me because she was smaller, quieter, and less overwhelming than the mega-ship alternative my children had suggested. I could find solitude when flashbacks surfaced or when crowded spaces triggered responses I'd rather not explain to strangers on vacation. The library, the aft deck at dawn, Viking Crown Lounge before the morning rush—these sanctuaries existed throughout the ship, always available, never requiring explanation or apology.

The crew treated me with professionalism that felt familiar—doing their jobs well, anticipating needs, not making a production of service. I recognized the dynamic from good sailors: competence delivered without self-congratulation. Our cabin steward learned my routine—early riser, late reader, coffee before conversation—and adapted without discussion. The dining staff remembered my preferences and my wife's habit of ordering for me when I was distracted by whatever memory the current port had triggered.

On the last night, I stood on the aft deck watching our wake dissolve into Mediterranean darkness. I'd spent thirty years watching wakes from warships—always heading somewhere with purpose, always on mission, always calculating arrival times and fuel consumption and threat assessments. This time, the wake meant nothing operational. It was just water disturbed by passage, returning to calm behind us. Beautiful. Purposeless. Free.

My wife found me there, as she always finds me when I've gone quiet. "You okay?" she asked—the question she'd asked ten thousand times across our marriage, each time genuinely wanting the answer.

"Better than okay," I said. "I finally got to enjoy it."

**The lesson:** The places we've served deserve to be experienced in peace—and the right ship provides not just transportation to those places but the specific quietude that allows a military mind to release its operational framework and simply witness beauty without assessing it.**

stories.title: The Final Sailing: When Splendour Left Royal Caribbean
stories.intended_reader: Those who sailed Splendour's final Royal Caribbean sailing in 2016
stories.core_insight: Splendour's final sailing brought together passengers who had formed lasting connections with the ship
stories.markdown:
**I sailed Splendour of the Seas' final Royal Caribbean cruise in April 2016, and I need to tell you from the start that I wasn't just saying goodbye to a ship. I was saying goodbye to the vessel that had carried my husband and me through eight Mediterranean voyages, three transatlantic crossings, and twenty years of memories that became infinitely more precious after Robert died in 2014 and I realized that the past was the only direction we'd ever travel together again.**

Robert and I discovered Splendour in 1997, one year after her launch, on a Mediterranean sailing from Barcelona to Rome. We were forty-three years old, recently empty-nested, and searching for something to fill the space our children's departure had created. The travel agent suggested cruising. Robert suggested Europe. The calendar suggested Splendour. Twenty years later, she'd become so interwoven with our marriage that separating the ship from the relationship required a kind of surgery I wasn't prepared to perform.

We sailed Splendour eight times over seventeen years. Mediterranean loops that became our annual pilgrimage—the ports we returned to like old friends, the crew who recognized us by our third voyage, the Viking Crown Lounge sunset that became our evening ritual across decades of sailing. Robert would order scotch, I'd order wine, and we'd watch the Mediterranean turn gold while discussing what we'd seen that day, what we'd see tomorrow, and the specific gratitude of two people who'd found the life they wanted and had the good sense to live it deliberately.

When Robert died in 2014—sudden cardiac arrest, no warning, no goodbye, no chance to say any of the things you'd say if you knew the end was approaching—I cancelled every future plan we'd made. Splendour's 2015 Mediterranean sailing. The transatlantic crossing we'd booked for spring. The vacation wardrobe he'd helped me select. Everything that reminded me of our shared future went into a box labeled "later" that really meant "never."

Then Royal Caribbean announced they were selling Splendour. The ship we'd loved for seventeen years was leaving the fleet. The final sailing was scheduled for April 2016 from Barcelona—our city, Robert's favorite embarkation port, the place where our Splendour story had begun.

I couldn't let the ship leave without me. Robert would have insisted I go. Our daughter insisted I go. My therapist insisted I go. Everyone who knew what Splendour meant to our marriage insisted with the collective urgency of people who understood that some goodbyes require presence even when presence means pain.

The ship was full of Splendour loyalists. People who'd sailed her dozens of times. Crew members who'd been aboard for years. Everyone knew this was the end, and the awareness created an atmosphere I've never experienced on any other sailing—tender, melancholic, fiercely alive. A couple from Germany introduced themselves at the Boleros bar. Twenty-three Splendour sailings. They spoke about the ship the way I spoke about Robert—with love that refused to accept that the object of their affection was departing permanently.

A British widow found me at dinner on the second night. Her husband had proposed in Viking Crown Lounge in 2003—dropped to one knee as the Mediterranean sunset blazed through 360 degrees of glass. She was sailing the final voyage because "leaving without saying goodbye felt like the cruelest kind of abandonment." I understood that sentence in the specific way that only people who've lost both a spouse and a ship in the same decade can understand.

A family from Australia occupied the table beside ours. They'd chosen Splendour for every European vacation for fifteen years—their children had grown up aboard this ship, measuring themselves against the doorframe of their favorite cabin the way other families measure against kitchen walls at home. Their teenage daughter cried at dinner the final night. "It's just a ship," her brother said. She shook her head. "It's not just a ship," she replied. She was right.

The crew seemed to move more slowly during that final sailing, savoring moments they'd normally rush through with professional efficiency. Turndown service took longer because our steward arranged things with deliberate care. Dinner service lingered because the waiters wanted to remember our faces. The captain's announcements carried historical weight—sharing Splendour's achievements, her records, her place in the fleet's evolution—with the specific pride of someone eulogizing a colleague.

On the final evening, I stood at Viking Crown Lounge in the spot where Robert always stood—port side, facing west, scotch position on the railing where his glass would rest. I'd brought a photograph of him, small enough for my jacket pocket, and I held it up to the glass so he could see the Mediterranean sunset one last time through the windows he'd loved for seventeen years.

In Barcelona, as passengers disembarked for the last time, crew members lined the gangway. Many had tears. They weren't just saying goodbye to passengers—they were saying goodbye to their shipboard home, the place where they'd spent years living and working in a community that was dissolving with every departing guest.

I touched the railing one last time. "Thank you," I whispered. "For everything. For him. For us."

Splendour sailed to TUI shortly after, becoming Marella Discovery. She's still out there—different name, different crew, same glass walls and Viking Crown Lounge. Sometimes I imagine Robert there still, scotch in hand, watching the sunset from our favorite spot, on a ship that carries his memory in ways that no new vessel ever could.

**The lesson:** The ships that carry our most precious memories become more than vessels—they become the architecture of love itself, and saying goodbye to them is saying goodbye to the places where we were most fully ourselves with the people who mattered most.**

stories.title: Life After Royal Caribbean: Splendour as Marella Discovery
stories.intended_reader: Travelers curious about where Splendour is now
stories.core_insight: Splendour continues sailing as Marella Discovery, preserving much of her original character
stories.markdown:
**Splendour of the Seas didn't disappear when Royal Caribbean sold her in 2016. She changed names, changed livery, and changed markets—but the glass walls remained, the Viking Crown Lounge remained, and the ship that had spent twenty years earning loyalty in Mediterranean waters continued sailing those same waters under a name most of her original passengers didn't recognize. This is the story of a ship's second life, told by someone who sailed both chapters.**

I first met Splendour in 2004 as a Royal Caribbean passenger on a Greek islands sailing. She was eight years old then, carrying the gentle wear of nearly a decade at sea, and I loved her immediately—the glass walls, the intimate scale, the Viking Crown Lounge sunsets that turned every evening into a private showing of Mediterranean light. I sailed her four more times before the 2016 sale, each voyage confirming that this particular ship suited European waters with a precision that newer, larger vessels couldn't replicate.

When TUI Cruises acquired Splendour for their Marella Cruises brand, I tracked the transformation with the particular intensity of someone watching a beloved home change ownership. The exterior colors shifted from Royal Caribbean's distinctive blue-and-white to Marella's blue-silver livery. Crown and Anchor logos yielded to Marella branding. Viking Crown Lounge received a new name—though the structure, the glass, and the 360-degree views remained untouched because you don't rename a sunset.

The onboard experience shifted to serve TUI's British market: all-inclusive pricing that bundled drinks and gratuities into the fare, British entertainment sensibilities replacing American cruise programming, dining that acknowledged British palate preferences while maintaining the general quality that the kitchen infrastructure supported. The changes were real but cosmetic—they altered what the ship served without altering what the ship was.

I sailed Marella Discovery in 2019, three years after the conversion, and the experience confirmed what I'd suspected: Splendour's bones determined her personality more than any rebranding could change. Walking the corridors felt familiar despite new carpet and different artwork. The Centrum atrium rose seven stories with the same glass elevators that had carried me between decks for fifteen years. The general layout—where things were, how spaces connected, the flow of public areas into private retreats—remained the architectural DNA that twenty years of Splendour identity had encoded.

The passengers were different—predominantly British rather than the international mix that Royal Caribbean attracted—but the demographics shared core characteristics. Experienced travelers. Couples and multigenerational families. People who preferred mid-sized ships for the same reasons Splendour loyalists always had: manageability, intimacy, access to ports that larger ships couldn't reach.

The observation lounge—whatever Marella calls it—still provided 360-degree views from the ship's highest public point. I sat there at sunset, Mediterranean light flooding the glass exactly as it had under the Royal Caribbean flag, and felt the particular bittersweetness of visiting a beloved place that's been redecorated by someone else. The furniture was different. The drinks menu was different. The sunset was identical—because sunsets don't change owners.

One moment crystallized the continuity beneath the changes. Walking the promenade deck at dawn, before other passengers had woken, I noticed a small plaque near the bridge that listed the ship's construction details: Chantiers de l'Atlantique, 1996. Splendour of the Seas. Her birth name, still physically present on the ship that wore a different name now, like a wedding ring kept in a drawer after remarriage—evidence of a previous life that still mattered.

For former Splendour passengers curious about sailing her again: Marella Discovery offers that opportunity. The ship has been updated but not fundamentally transformed. Walking the decks, watching sunset from the observation lounge, dining in the main restaurant while Mediterranean coastlines slide past the windows—these experiences remain available to anyone willing to accept that a beloved ship wearing a new name is still, beneath the surface, the ship you loved.

She's thirty years old now. Still sailing. Still glass-wrapped. Still carrying passengers through European waters with the particular grace of a mid-sized ship that fits the ports she visits rather than overwhelming them.

Some ships are scrapped when they leave major cruise lines. Splendour received a second life. She deserved it—and she's living it well.

**The lesson:** Ships, like people, carry their essential identity through changes of name and circumstance—and the passengers who knew them first can recognize that identity in the glass walls, the sunset views, and the unchanged bones that no rebranding can alter.**
